title: Full-Cycle Training Simulation
description: Walk through a complete end-to-end sales scenario from lead intake through customer activation.

sections.title: Full-Cycle Training Simulation
sections.content:
You've learned the pieces. Now let's watch them work together. Here's a real deal flowing through the NxN system—from first contact to active customer—and why each step matters.

The Scenario: Acme Financial Services

Acme Financial Services. 500 employees. Three offices. Mid-market company exploring a digital transformation.

Sarah Chen, Director of Operations, discovers NxN online. She fills out an interest form. A new lead is born.

Stage 1: Lead Intake

The system captures the basics: Sarah Chen, Acme Financial Services, sarah.chen@acmefinancial.com, source (website inquiry).

Before the lead saves, duplicate matching runs:
- Email in system? No.
- Company in system? No.
- Result: New prospect confirmed.

The lead auto-assigns to Michael Torres (regional sales manager, financial services specialist).

Stage 2: Verification

Michael acts immediately.

- Calls Sarah to confirm she owns this evaluation
- Verifies Acme is real (checks website, confirms details)
- Asks about current challenges, team structure, budget appetite
- Sarah shares: Early-stage digital transformation. Potential budget in 6 months.

Michael updates the lead: "Contacted" → "Verified." Documents the conversation.

Stage 3: Qualification

Michael and Sarah have two more calls. A Solutions Architect (Alex) joins to dig into technical fit.

What emerges:
- Need: Consolidate three legacy systems (accounting, CRM, reporting). Manual reconciliation every month. Painful, error-prone.
- Scope: ~50 daily users. Integration to treasury system.
- Budget: $750K–$1.5M preliminary allocation.
- Timeline: Decision in 4 months. Go-live Q3 next year.

All five gates met. Michael marks the lead "Qualified."

Stage 4: Lead Conversion & Hierarchy

Michael converts the lead. The system checks: Is there a Parent Account for Acme? No.

The system creates:
- Parent Account: "Acme Financial Services" (the company)
- Entity Child Account: "Acme Financial Services - Core Platform Project" (the business unit)

Sarah converts to a Contact linked to the Parent Account. Her role and decision authority are documented.

Stage 5: Account & Stakeholder Setup

Michael and Jennifer (CSM) add Acme's other stakeholders:
- James Rodriguez, CFO (budget authority)
- Priya Patel, IT Director (technical requirements)
- David Okonkwo, Business Process Lead (end-user voice)

Parent Account gets enriched: company size, HQ (New York), industry, website, annual revenue.

Classification: "Strategic Prospect" (size + long-term potential).

Stage 6: Opportunity Creation

Michael creates the opportunity on the Entity Child Account (critical).

Opportunity: "Acme Financial Services - Integrated Platform Implementation - $1.2M"

- Close Date: 4 months
- Stage: Qualification
- Amount: $1.2M (50 users × scope)
- Probability: 20% (early exploration, no budget confirmation yet)
- Primary Contact: Sarah Chen
- Next Steps: Technical deep-dive with Alex

Stage 7: Solution Definition → Pricing

Alex and Priya meet. Technical fit is strong.

Michael moves the opportunity to "Solution Definition."
- Gate check: ✓ Contact confirmed, ✓ next steps clear, ✓ value understood ($300K annual efficiency gain)
- Probability: 40%

Michael and the Sales Engineer build a detailed quote:
- Core Platform: 50 users × $18K/user/year = $900K annual
- Integration services: $300K one-time
- Process consulting: $100K
- Total Year 1: $1.3M

The quote goes to Pricing Approver. Approved. Sent to Sarah.

Opportunity stage: "Pricing & Quotation." Probability: 60%.

Stage 8: Negotiation & Customer Review

Sarah reviews the proposal. She wants payment flexibility: can they do annual upfront instead of quarterly?

Michael negotiates with Finance. They agree: 3% discount for annual upfront.

Revised quote prepared, approved, and sent.

Sarah: "Looks great. Let's talk implementation timeline and success metrics."

Stage: "Customer Review." Probability: 60%.

Stage 9: Contracting

Legal prepares a Statement of Work (SOW) and Master Service Agreement (MSA):
- 4-month implementation. Go-live Q3.
- 24×7 support. 99.5% uptime SLA.
- Payment: $1.3M upfront before kickoff.
- Cancellation: 90-day notice (immediate on non-payment).

Sarah's legal team reviews. They request two changes:
1. 60-day implementation instead of 4 months (more aggressive)
2. Change order process for scope changes

NxN Legal agrees. Modified contract approved and sent.

Sarah's CFO and CIO review and sign electronically.

Opportunity stage: "Contracting." Probability: 90%.

Stage 10: Closed Won

Contract is fully signed. Michael closes the opportunity "Won."

Reason documented: "Strong technical fit. Business outcomes align. Customer trusts our implementation approach."

Stage 11: Onboarding Transition

The Parent Account moves from "Prospect" to "Onboarding."

Jennifer (CSM) takes over as Account Manager.

Onboarding checklist created:
- IT infrastructure & access confirmed
- Implementation kickoff scheduled
- PM and extended team assigned
- Data migration plan (legacy systems)
- User training scheduled
- Environment and security configured

Stage 12: Quote & Contract Linking

In Salesforce, the accepted Quote (Version 2) and executed Contract are linked:
- Final value: $1.3M annually, 3% upfront discount applied
- Payment confirmed

Revenue recognition begins (Year 1 = $1.3M upon contract execution and payment receipt).

Stage 13: Implementation & Go-Live

Over 4 months, the project team and Acme work through:
- Data migration from three legacy systems
- Workflow configuration and security setup
- User training and best practices
- User acceptance testing
- Issue resolution

Accme's team stays engaged. Go-live hits the timeline.

Stage 14: Customer Activation

Metrics confirm success: 45+ daily active users. Key transactions flowing smoothly.

Jennifer moves the Account to "Active."

Acme is now a paying customer generating recurring revenue.

Jennifer begins:
- Quarterly business reviews (QBRs)
- Usage monitoring and satisfaction tracking
- Identifying expansion: additional modules, more user seats, advanced reporting

The Complete Flow

1. Interest form submitted → Lead created
2. Verify legitimacy → Qualified
3. Gather requirements → Convert to Parent + Child Account
4. Add stakeholders → Setup complete
5. Create opportunity on Entity Child → Initial $1.2M estimate
6. Technical deep-dive → Solution Definition (40% prob)
7. Build & approve quote → Pricing & Quotation (60% prob)
8. Negotiate terms → Customer Review (60% prob)
9. Draft, revise & sign contract → Contracting (90% prob)
10. Sign fully → Closed Won
11. Transition to CSM → Onboarding begins
12. 4-month implementation → Go-live
13. Usage validates success → Active customer

This is the NxN process in motion. Every stage has gates. Every role owns their work. Data stays clean. The result: transparent pipeline, accurate forecasts, clear audit trail.

When you execute this consistently, you deliver three outcomes: consistency (everyone speaks the same language), control (governance protects the company), and visibility (clean data enables good decisions).

keyTakeaways: Real deals move through all stages systematically: Lead Intake → Verification → Qualification → Conversion → Opportunity → Contracting → Won
Each role (Sales Rep, CSM, Legal, Finance, Approvers) owns specific responsibilities at each stage
Proper execution of the end-to-end process ensures transparent pipeline, accurate forecasting, and clear audit trail
